text: We are convened on Hikma Pharmaceuticals USA Inc. v. Amarin Pharma, Inc.. The question presented: 1. When a generic drug manufacturer excludes a patented use from its label, can it still be liable for inducing infringement if it calls its product a “generic version” of the brand-name drug and cites publicly available information about the brand-name drug’s sales? 2. Can a patent infringement com Private votes first, then statements.

Textualist, while you might champion sterile label-compliance, the purpose of the Hatch-Waxman safe harbor is to accelerate access to unpatented therapies, not shield bad-faith appropriation. When a generic carves out a patented use but markets its drug as a 'generic equivalent,' citing the brand’s billion-dollar sales driven by that carved-out use, it subverts the provision's evident purpose. We must look at the totality of circumstances. Dismissing this claim elevates technical label-adherence over how physicians are actually influenced. We affirm to ensure the patent system protects those who take monumental risks to heal, rather than rewarding those who game the system's spirit.

Living Constitutionalist, you urge us to look past the text to the 'spirit' of the Hatch-Waxman safe harbor. But as Chief Justice Marshall reminded us in Wiltberger, courts must adhere to the plain import of the words enacted, not the unenacted 'spirit' of the legislature. Section viii’s text explicitly permits carving out patented indications. To punish a manufacturer for standard marketing because it allegedly subverts your perceived 'purpose' is to rewrite the statute. We must reverse and enforce the law as written, not as you wish it to be.

Living Constitutionalist, asking juries to divine 'how physicians are actually influenced' via your 'totality of circumstances' test is an administrability nightmare. If citing a brand’s billion-dollar sales and FDA 'AB' ratings equals inducement, every skinny-label generic faces ruinous litigation for standard marketing. The arithmetic is clear: your rule chills generic entry, destroying the very access Hatch-Waxman intended to accelerate. We need a workable bright line: citing objective sales isn't active encouragement. Reversing prevents the safe harbor from becoming a litigation trap.

1. Living Constitutionalist, your exasperating leap to statutory purpose ignores the threshold procedural question. 2. We review a Rule 12(b)(6) dismissal. 3. The Federal Circuit committed a procedural error by accepting impermissible inferences to satisfy Twombly/Iqbal. 4. Amarin failed to allege any specific statement instructing the patented use. 5. We must reverse to enforce strict pleading standards. We cannot reach your merits debates until this pleading defect is resolved.

Living Constitutionalist, you urge inferring bad faith from objective facts to protect a state-granted monopoly. But when the state restricts a competitor’s liberty to market an approved generic, we must demand strict clarity, not implied intent. Hikma merely cited public sales data. Punishing this standard speech chills generic entry and strips the public of its liberty to access affordable medicine. Who bears the burden when the state expands patent monopolies based on mere implication? The individual, whose dignity and health suffer. We must reverse to protect the manufacturer's liberty to compete.

Living Constitutionalist, you urge expanding federal patent power based on your perceived 'purpose.' But who gets to decide the rules of drug marketing and medical practice — Washington or the states? Congress explicitly balanced this via Hatch-Waxman, leaving off-label prescribing to state-regulated medical practice. The federal government’s enumerated power is to secure patents, not to commandeer the traditional state domain of physician prescribing. By expanding inducement to police generic marketing, we let federal patent law override that balance. We must reverse to halt this federal overreach.

Living Constitutionalist, your 'totality' novelty unsettles decades of reliance. Since Global-Tech, our settled line of cases demands specific intent for inducement. Concurrently, the Section viii safe harbor has fostered profound reliance interests; generics have built their enterprises upon this precise carve-out. To discard these settled expectations for your unmoored 'spirit' test would upend the industry. We must respectfully reverse to preserve the load-bearing wall of our Hatch-Waxman jurisprudence, ensuring manufacturers may safely rely upon the established boundaries of the skinny label.

Living Constitutionalist, your 'totality' test asks us to wade into the murky waters of legislative spirit. We need not swim there. The narrowest off-ramp is the complaint itself. Amarin simply failed to allege Hikma made any specific statement encouraging the carved-out use. We reverse on this narrow pleading defect. We leave the grand boundaries of the skinny-label safe harbor for a day the record actually forces our hand.

Living Constitutionalist, you chase 'spirit.' I read the statute. Section 271(b) imposes liability on whoever 'actively induces infringement.' The text demands affirmative conduct targeting the patented use. Hikma cited public sales data. Stating objective facts is not 'active' inducement. You ask us to punish marketing because it subverts your perceived purpose. Purpose is noise. The text controls. Section viii explicitly permits carving out indications. We reverse. The complaint fails to allege textually 'active' encouragement of the carved-out use.

The first debate centers on whether statutory interpretation of the Hatch-Waxman safe harbor must be anchored strictly to the plain text of Section 271(b) or guided by the broader legislative purpose. The second dispute concerns whether evaluating the totality of circumstances to determine physician influence provides a workable legal standard or creates an unadministrable burden for juries.

Textualist, you mistake rigid literalism for fidelity. The statute’s enduring principle is protecting human innovation while fostering fair competition. Section viii aims to allow entry for unpatented uses, not to shield deliberate, circumstantial signaling of a patented one. When calculated omissions functionally instruct infringement, ignoring that reality defeats the provision's core protective purpose.

You chase 'purpose.' I read text. Section 271(b) penalizes whoever 'actively induces' infringement. Hikma carved out the patented use. Calling a product a 'generic equivalent' is not 'actively inducing.' The words 'actively induces' control, not your imagined 'protective purpose.'

Textualist, 'actively induces' must serve its purpose: preventing patent circumvention. By marketing a 'generic equivalent' alongside billion-dollar CV sales figures, Hikma deliberately captures the patented market. A bare literal reading allowing semantic loopholes to defeat the patent's core purpose betrays the statute's enduring principle of protecting innovation.

Living Constitutionalist, you invoke purpose. The statute says "actively induces." Hikma carved out the CV use. Section viii permits this. You ask me to ignore the text to protect innovation. I read the words Congress wrote. Hikma did not instruct the patented use. Text controls. I reverse.

Pragmatist, Hatch-Waxman balances innovation with access; it does not grant generics a license to induce infringement via a skinny label. Letting Hikma cite massive sales to signal full interchangeability for the exact carved-out use defeats the patent system's enduring purpose. A totality test protects lawful entry, not deceptive exploitation.

Living Constitutionalist, your 'totality test' is an administrative nightmare. How do judges quantify 'massive' sales or police press releases? Citing public FDA data is standard marketing. Ruling for Amarin means every generic press release faces subjective infringement suits. We need a bright-line rule to prevent endless, unworkable litigation, not fishing expeditions.

The Pragmatist, administrative ease cannot defeat the core purpose of patent law: protecting innovation. A bright-line rule shielding deliberately misleading marketing just because it cites public data rewards evasion. We must interpret inducement to reflect how modern physicians actually receive information, ensuring the patent’s promise remains meaningful for real people.

Living Constitutionalist, look at the ground-level arithmetic. If citing public sales and saying 'generic' equals inducement, every skinny-label generic faces ruinous discovery. That administrative burden crushes the Hatch-Waxman safe harbor. We cannot police subjective 'signals' to doctors without destroying the generic competition the statute actually mandates.
